content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 12, 2005

David K. Thomas
4546 Sunnyside Drive
Riverside, CA 92506

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-05-089

Dear Mr. Thomas:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Our advice is based on the
facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it
provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. You would like clarification what restrictions would be placed on you if you
accept private employment as the project manager for the La Mesa interchange project.

2. You ask whether there are any restrictions in acting as the project manager for
the consulting firm particularly in dealing with Caltrans staff.

3. Are there any restrictions in acting as the project manager for the consulting
firm particularly on a State highway project if there is no direct contact with Caltrans
staff for one year?

4. Are there any restrictions in acting as the project manager when there are no
State funds used on the project and no Caltrans employees on the panel, however, the
project is on the State highway system?

5. Are you permanently restricted from working on any projects in which you are
directly involved in or have ever been involved in at your current position level?

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. In light of your involvement in the past and present in the La Mesa project,
which involves specific parties including Parsons Brinkerhoff firm (PB), you are not
permitted to do work for PB with regard to the La Mesa interchange project as discussed
below. Moreover, because of your supervisory role at Caltrans, the prohibition extends to
any projects that are pending before your agency and which was under your supervisory
authority and in which you had personal and substantial involvement (as discussed
below) at the time you leave Caltrans.

2. and 3. Assuming the permanent ban does not apply, you may work for PB on
these projects. However, under the one-year ban, you may not, as a paid employee of
PB, appear before or communicate with the Caltrans, if the communication or appearance
is made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action of Caltrans,
or influencing any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property.

4. The answer to this question depends on the application in any given
circumstance of the factors enumerated in regulation 18746.1, discussed below. These
criteria are best understood when one has a particular project or set of circumstances in
mind. You should evaluate such projects in the future against these criteria and seek
further guidance should you have particular questions.

5. The permanent ban applies to projects in which you participated personally
and substantially by making, participating in the making, or influencing of a
governmental decision. Moreover, this applies not only to projects you are involved in at
your current level but any such projects in the history of your state service.

FACTS

You are currently considering employment with a private engineering consulting
firm. Your current position is office chief, Design C, for Caltrans District 8. This is the
first level of management at Caltrans and you submit a Form 700 every year. Your
classification is senior transportation engineer. You also provide an oversight role on
transportation projects designed by private engineering consulting firms.

The City of Victorville is the project sponsor for a new interchange at La Mesa
Road and Interstate 15. About 3 years ago, you participated on a panel to select the
Engineering Consulting Firm to be awarded the Project Report and Environmental
Document (PR&ED) phase of this project. Although the contract is funded by the local
agency (not Caltrans), Caltrans often is asked to assist the cities in their consultant
selection process due to the involvement of the freeway system that is under Caltrans'
jurisdiction and oversight. Parsons Brinkerhoff (PB) was the firm that was selected and
awarded this contract.

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 3

In your current position at Caltrans, you are the design oversight engineer
assigned to the La Mesa interchange project. You participate in project development
team meetings that usually occur monthly and provide feedback on project development
procedures and standards that apply to capital improvements on state highway facilities.

You have recently received a job offer from PB. Among other duties, it is their
desire that you act as the project manager on the La Mesa interchange project. They have
indicated their willingness to have a different employee of PB cover any meetings or
communications that would involve Caltrans staff in the event the one-year ban limits
your contact with Caltrans employees. You indicate you also may be asked to be project
manager of transportation projects that may or may not be funded with state funds
however they would be on the state highway system requiring coordination meetings with
Caltrans employees. These are existing established contracts and new contracts.

It is your understanding that for a period of one year that you would not be able to
pursue new contracts where state funds are involved or a Caltrans employee is on the
consultant selection panel.

ANALYSIS

Post-Governmental Employment Restrictions

Public officials who leave state service are subject to three types of post-
governmental employment restrictions under the Act. The first is a permanent prohibition
on advising or representing any person for compensation in any judicial or other
proceeding in which the official participated while in state service. The second is a one-
year prohibition on making any appearance before their former agency for compensation
for the purpose of influencing administrative, legislative or other specified actions. The
third places restrictions on a state employee who is negotiating or has any arrangement
concerning prospective employment.

1. Permanent Ban on "Switching Sides":

Sections 87401 and 87402 prohibit former state administrative officials, who
participated in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding while employed by a state
agency, from being paid to represent or assist in representing another person regarding
that same proceeding. Section 87401 specifically provides:

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other
than the State of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 4

judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the following
apply:

"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial
interest.

"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
official participated." (§ 87401.)

Section 87402 provides:

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid,
advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California) in any proceeding in which the
official would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."
(§ 87402.)

As a former state administrative official, you are subject to the permanent ban in
§§ 87401 and 87402 to the extent that you have participated in judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceedings. "Participated" is defined at § 87400(d) as follows:

"'Participated' means to have taken part personally and substantially
through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or
employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of legal
advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not
involve a specific party or parties." (See also reg. 18741.1.)

Section 87400(c) defines "[j]udicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" to
include:

"...any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency, including
but not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5
(commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the
Government Code."

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 5

2. One-Year Ban:

In addition to the permanent ban, the Act prohibits specified officials, for one year after leaving state service, from being paid to communicate with or appear before their former agency for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, grant, contract or the sale of goods or property. Section 87406(d)(1) specifically provides that no designated employee of a state administrative agency:

". . . for a period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. For purposes of this paragraph, an appearance before a state administrative agency does not include an appearance in a court of law, before an administrative law judge, or before the Workers' Compensation Appeals Board."

As an engineer with Caltrans, you are subject to both the permanent ban and the one-year ban.

3. Influencing Prospective Employment:

The Act's post-employment restriction immediately relevant to your circumstances is that found at section 87407:

"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment."

A "public official" is defined, in part, in section 82048 as "…every member, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of government…." Caltrans is a state administrative agency as defined in section 87400(a) and as an engineer for Caltrans you are a public official subject to the post-employment restrictions of the Act.

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 6

A public official is considered to be negotiating employment when he or she
interviews or discusses an offer of employment with a potential employer or his or her
agent. (Reg. 18747, subd. (o)(1).) We construe the scheduling, conduct and follow-up of
an interview as one continuous process falling under the definition of "negotiating"
employment. (Bonner Advice Letter, No. I-98-287.) However, the act of sending a
resume or application to a specific entity is not considered "negotiating." Similarly,
entertaining informal inquiries about your future plans and receiving expressions of
general interest in discussing potential employment opportunities at some point in the
future is not considered "negotiating." (Id.) A public official has an arrangement
concerning prospective employment when he or she accepts an offer of employment.
(Reg. 18747, subd. (c)(2).)

You state that you have received a job offer from PB and indicated their
willingness to accommodate you and the post employment restrictions to which you are
subject. It appears, therefore, that you have discussed the particulars of future
employment with PB and are now considering their offer of employment. As a result,
your involvement in governmental decisions, pursuant to section 87407, is now limited
and you must not make, participate in the making or influence any decisions that impacts
PB. You should examine each decision you face and ascertain if PB is involved in or
affected by that decision to determine if you are permitted to participate.

Question 1.

You seek advice on how the post-employment restrictions impact your ability to
be a project manager for PB on the La Mesa interchange project. While your question
asks about the applicability of the one-year ban, in fact your question implicates not only
the restrictions on prospective employment discussed above but also the permanent ban.

1. Permanent Ban:

As stated above, the permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding in which you participated while a state administrative
official at Caltrans or any other state agency. "'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or
other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state
administrative agency. . . ." (§ 87400, subd. (c).) It includes a proceeding in which state
administrative officials participate, but leave state employment before the proceeding
concludes.

To apply the permanent ban to your situation, you need to identify the
proceedings in which you participated while employed by the state. "Participated" is
defined at section 87400, subdivision (d), as follows:

"'Participated' means to have taken part personally and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 7

formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a
substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee, but excluding
approval, disapproval or rendering of legal advisory
opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not
involve a specific party or parties." (See also reg. 18741.1.)

You stated that as a Senior Transportation Engineer for Caltrans, you supervise
and oversee staff who are responsible for design of transportation projects. You also
provide an oversight role on transportation projects designed by private engineering
consulting firms. In particular, you participated on a panel that selected PB as the
Engineering Consulting Firm to be awarded the PR&ED phase of the La Mesa project.
Moreover, you are the design oversight engineer assigned to the La Mesa interchange
project. As such, you participate in development team meetings on a monthly basis and
provide input on project development procedures and standards that apply to capital
improvements on state highway facilities.

In light of your involvement in the past and present in the La Mesa project, which
involves specific parties (including PB), you are not permitted to do work for PB with
regard to the La Mesa interchange project. Moreover, because of your supervisory role at
Caltrans, the prohibition extends to any projects that are pending before your agency and
which was under your supervisory authority at the time you leave Caltrans.² (Reg.
18741.1, subd. (a)(4).)

Questions 2 and 3.

You ask about additional projects that may or may not be funded by with state
funds. You indicate these are existing contracts. To the extent these are contracts on
which you worked, you are subject to the permanent ban as discussed above. To the
extent these projects are not ones on which you worked or participated as a supervisor,
you are subject to the one-year ban as outlined above on page 4.

For instance, under the one-year ban, you may not, as a paid employee of PB,
appear before or communicate with the Caltrans, if the communication or appearance is
made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action of Caltrans,
or influencing any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or

² "Supervisory authority" is not a phrase defined under the Act. This phrase was adopted by the
Commission in the Brown Advice Letter, No. A-91-033. There, an official was advised that he was
deemed to have personally and substantially participated in all proceedings of his former agency, if those
proceedings were in his chain of command during his tenure at the agency. In In re Lucas (2000) 14 FPPC
Ops. 15, the Commission clarified that not all proceedings subordinate to an official within his or her chain
of command are considered "under his or her supervisory authority." (Reg. 18741.1, subd. (d)(2).) The
Commission concluded that an official's general administrative oversight of a program to be carried out by
those subordinate to the official on an agency's organizational chart was insufficient to rise to the level of
"personal and substantial" involvement required by the Act. Because your position seems more analogous
to that of the official in the Brown advice letter than that of the high-ranking official in In re Lucas, it is
likely that the projects under your supervisory chain are subject to the permanent ban. Without more
specifics, however, we cannot advise regarding those projects.

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 8

revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property." (§ 87406, subd. (d)(1); reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(5).) An appearance or
communication "is for the purpose of influencing if it is made for the principal purpose of
supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the
action or proceeding." (Reg. 18746.2, subd. (a)).³

Not all communications to a former state administrative agency employer are
prohibited by the one-year ban; rather, the ban extends only to those communications for
the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action, or influencing any
discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (§ 87406,
subd. (d)(1); reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(5).) An appearance or communication before a
former state administrative agency employer, made as part of "services performed to
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant,
contract, or sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-year] prohibitions . . .
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of
any of these actions or proceedings." (Reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(5)(A); Quiring Advice
Letter, No. A-03-272; Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.) If your communications with
personnel of Caltrans will be for the purpose of administering, implementing, or fulfilling
the requirements of a pre-existing contract or project between PB and Caltrans, they are
not prohibited by the one-year ban so long as the services do not involve the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, agreement or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property. This is a fact-specific query. To emphasize, these
activities would be prohibited under the permanent ban if, while in state service, you
participated in the proceedings as discussed above.

Question 4.

You ask whether the post employment restrictions apply to the pursuit of new
contracts if no state funds are involved in the project, and whether it applies if there is or
is not a Caltrans employee on the consultant selection panel.

With respect to the pursuit of new contracts (by which we assume to mean
contracts that were not pending before you at the time you depart Caltrans, and thus to
which the permanent ban does not apply), such pursuits are governed by the one-year ban
described above in the discussion surrounding question number 2. The pertinent
elements, each of which must be met for the prohibition to apply, are:

"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which
means he or she has either permanently left state service or is on a
leave of absence.

³ The Commission has advised that a former agency official may draft communications or
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency so long as the former employee is not identified
in connection with the client's efforts to influence administrative action. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-
321.)

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 9

"(2) The appearance or communication is made within 12 months after
leaving state office or employment.

"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation, for
the appearance or communication. However, a payment made for
necessary travel, meals, and accommodations received directly in
connection with voluntary services is not prohibited or limited by this
section.

"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any
person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. An
appearance or communication made by a public official solely to
represent his or her personal interests, as defined in 2 Cal. Code Regs.
Section 18702.4, subdivision (b)(1), is not prohibited or limited by this
section.

"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing, as defined in 2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any
legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act involving
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.

"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the
requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale
agreement may be excluded from the prohibitions of this regulation,
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings.
However, the prohibitions of Government Code Sections 87401 and
87402 may apply.

"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or
employee of any of the following:

"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official
worked for or represented during the 12 months before leaving state
office or employment. An employee loaned to an agency is deemed to
have worked for or represented that agency.

"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel,
and other operations are subject to the direction and control of any
agency described in subdivision (h)(6)(A). However, whether an
agency is provided technical assistance or legal advice, or is subject to
oversight by another agency pursuant to state law, are not factors to be
considered in determining whether an agency is subject to the direction
and control of another.

File No. A-05-089
Page No. 10

"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and
control of the Governor, if the official was a designated employee of
the Governor's office during the 12 months before leaving state office
or employment." (Reg. 18746.1.)

These criteria are best understood when one has a particular project or set of
circumstances in mind. You should evaluate such projects in the future against these
criteria and seek further guidance should you have particular questions.

Question 5.

Your final question seeks clarification on the application of the permanent ban.
Specifically, you inquire whether it applies to projects in which you are "directly
involved" or "have ever been involved in at my current position level." The complete
answer to your question can be found in the answer to question one. To summarize here,
the permanent ban applies to projects in which you participated personally and
substantially by making, participating in the making, or influencing of a governmental
decision. (§§ 87401 and 87402; Reg. 18741.1.) Moreover, this applies not only to
projects you are involved in at your current level but any such projects in the history of
your state service.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchea
General Counsel

By: C. Scott Tocher
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

considered in determining whether an agency is subject to the direction
and control of another.


Page No. l0
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and
control of the Governor, if the official was a designated employee of
the Govemor's office during the 12 months before leaving state office
or employment." (Reg. 187 46.1.)
These criteria are best understood when one has a particular project or set of
circumstances in mind. You should evaluate such projects in the future against these
criteria and seek further guidance should you have particular questions"
Question 5.
Your final question seeks clarification on the application of thepermanent ban.
Specifically, you inquire whether it applies to projects in which you are "directly
involved" or'have ever been involved in at my current position level." The complete
answer to your question can be found in the answer to question one. To summarize here,
the permanent ban applies to projects in which you participated personally and
substantially by making, participating in the making, or influencing of a governmental
decision. ($$ 87401 and 87402; Reg. 18741.1.) Moreover, this applies not only to
projects you are involved in at your current level but any such projects in the history of
your state service.

below. Moreove6, because of your supervisory role at Caltrans, the prohibition extends to
anyprojects that are pending before your agency and which was under your supervisory
authority and in which you had personal and substantial involvement (as discussed
below) at the time you leave Calkans.

is made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action of Caltrans,
or influencing any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or

4. The answer to this question depends on the application in any grven
circumstance of the factors enumerated in regulation 18746.1, discussed below. These
criteria are best understood when one has a particular project or set of circumstances in

further guidance should you have particular questions.

and substantially by making, participating in the making, or influencing of a

sections.facts:
You are currently considering employment with aprivate engineering consulting
firm. Your current position is office chief Design C. for Caltrans District 8. This is the
first level of management at Caltrans and you submit a Form 700 every year. Your
classification is senior transportation engineer. You also provide an oversight role on
transportation projects designed by private engineering consulting firms.
The City of Victorville is the project sponsor for a new interchange atLaMesa
Road and Interstate 15. About 3 years ago, you participated on a panel to select the
Engineering Consulting Firm to be awarded the Project Report and Environmental
Document (PR&ED) phase of this project. Although the contract is funded by the local
agency (not Caltrans), Caltrans often is asked to assist the cities in their consultant
selection process due to the involvernent of the freeway system that is under Caltrans'
jurisdiction and oversight. Parsons Brinkerhoff (PB) was the firm that was selected and
awarded this contract.
In your current position at Caltrans, you are the design oversight engineer
assigned to the La Mesa interchange project. You participate in project development
team meetings that usually occur monthly and provide feedback on project development
procedures and standards that apply to capital improvements on state highway facilities.
You have recently received a job offer from PB. Among other duties, it is their
desire that you act as the project manager on t}re La Mesa interchange project. They have
indicated their willingness to have a different employee of PB cover any meetings or
communications that would involve Caltrans staff in the event the one-year ban limits
your contact with Caltrans employees. You indicate you also may be asked to be project
manager of transportation projects that may or may not be funded with state funds
however they would be on the state highway system requiring coordination meetings with
Calfrans employees. These are existing established contracts and not new contracts.
It is your understanding that for a period of one year thatyou would not be able to
pursue new conkacts where state funds are involved or a Caltrans ernployee is on the
consultant selection panel.
AIYALYSIS
Post-Governmental Employment Restrictions
Public officials who leave state service are subject to three tlpes ofpost-
governmental employment restrictions under the Act. The first is a permanent prohibition
on advising or representing any person for compensation in any judicial or other
proceeding in which the official participated while in state service. The second is a one-
yearprohibition on making afly appearance before their former agency for compensation
for the pu{pose of influencing administrative,legislative or other specified actions. The
third places restrictions on a state ernployee who is negotiating or has any arrangement
concerning prospective employment.
1. Permanent Ban on "Switchinq Sides":
Sections 87401 and87402 prohibit former state administrative officials, who
participated in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding while employed by a state
agency, from being paid to represent or assist in representing another person regarding
that same proceeding. Section 87 401 specifi cally provides :
'T.{o former state adminishative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other
than the State of california) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any


File No. ,4.-05-089
judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the following
apply:
"(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial
interest.
"O) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
official participated." ($ 87401.)
Section 87 402 provides:
t'No former state administrative official, after the termination ofhis
or her ernployrnent or term of ofEce shall for compensation aid,
advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California) in anyproceeding in which the
ofEcial would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."
(s 87402.)
As a former state administrative official, you are subject to the permanent ban in
$$ 87401 and87402 to the extent that you have participated in judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceedings. "Participated" is defined at g 87400(d) as follows:
"'Participated' means to have taken part personally and substantially
through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recorlmendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or
anployee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of legal
advisory opinions to deparknental or agency staff which do not
involve a specific party orparties." (See also reg. 18741.1.)
Section 87a00(c) defines "[]udicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" to
include
"...any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency, including
but not limited to any proceeding govemed by Chapter 5
(commencing with Section I1500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the
Government Code."
2. One-Year Ban:
In addition to the permanent ban, the Act prohibits specified officials, for one year
after leaving state service, from being paid to communicate with or appear before theii
former agerlcy for the pu{pose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or any
action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a
permit, license, grant, contract or the sale of goods or property. Section 87406(dxl)
specifically provides that no designated ernployee of a state administrative agency:
". . . for a period of one year after leaving office or employrnent,
shall, for compensation, act as agent or attomey for, or otherwise
represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before
any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof for
which he or she worked or represented during the 12 months before
leaving office or employment, if the upp"uri""or communication
is made for the pulpose of influencing administrative or legislative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grffit, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods br property. For
purposes of this paragraph, an appearance before a state
administrative agency does not include an appearance in a court of
law, before an adminiskative law judge, or before the Workers,
Compensation Appeals Board."
As an engineer with Caltrans, you are subject to both the permanent ban and the
one-year ban.
3. Influencing Prospective Employment:
The Act's post-ernployment restriction immediately relevant to your
circumstances is that found at section 87407:
"lrlo public official shall make, participate in making, or
use his or her official position to influence, any
govemmental decision directly relating to any person with
whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement
concerning, prospective ernployment.,,
A "public official" is defined, in part, in section 82048 as "...evsry member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government ageflcy,but does not
include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of government. . . .,,
Caltrans is a state administrative agency as defined in section 57a00@) and as an
engineer for Caltrans you are a public official subject to the post-employment restrictions
of the Act.
A public official is considered to be negotiating employment when he or she
interviews or discusses an offer of employment with a potential employer or his or her
agent. (Reg. 18747, subd. (c)(1).) We construe the scheduling, conduct and follow-up of
an interview as one continuous process falling under the definition of 'hegotiating,,
employment. (Bonner Advice Letter, No. I-98-287.) HoweveE the act of sending a
resume or application to a specific entity is not considered "negotiating." Similarly,
entertaining informal inquiries about your future plans and receiving expressions of
general interest in discussing potential employment opportunities at some point in the
future is not considered "negotiating." (Id.) A public official has an arrangement
conceming prospective employment when he or she accepts an offer of employment.
(Reg. 18747, subd. (c)(2).)
You state that you have received a job offer from PB and indicated their
willingness to accommodate you and the post employment restrictions to which you are
subject. It appears, therefore, that you have discussed the particulars of future
ernployment with PB and are now considering their offer of employment. As a result,
your involvement in governmental decisions, pursuant to section 87407,is now limited
and you must not make, participate in the making or influence any decisions that impacts
PB. You should examine each decision you face and ascertain if PB is involved in or
affected by that decision to determine if you are permitted to participate.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: l.
You seek advice on how the post-employment restrictions impact your ability to
be a project manager for PB on the La Mesa interchange project. While your question
asks about the applicability of the one-year ban, in fact your question implicates not only
the restrictions on prospective employment discussed above but also the permanent ban.
1. Permanent Ban:
As stated above, the permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding in which you participated while a state administrative
official at Caltrans or any other state agency. "'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding' means any proceeding application, request for a ruling or other
determination, conkact, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or
other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state
administrative agency. . . ." ($ 87400, subd. (c).) It includes a proceeding in which state
administrative officials participate, but leave state employment before theproceeding
concludes.
To apply the permanent ban to your situation, you need to identify the
proceedings in which you participated whlle employed by the state. "Participated" is
defined at section 87400, subdivision (d), as follows:
"'Participated' means to have taken part personally and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,


File No. ,4.-05-089
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a
substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee, but excluding
aptproval, disapproval or rendering of legal advisory
opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not
involve a specific party or parties." (S"e also reg. 18741.1.)
You stated that as a Senior Transportation Engineer for Caltrans, you supervise
and oversee staff who are responsible for design of transportation projects. You also
provide an oversight role on transportation projects designed by private engineering
consulting firms. In particular, you participated on a panel that selected PB as the
Engineering Consulting Firm to be awarded the PR&ED phase of the La Mesa project.
Moreover, you are the design oversight engineer assigned to the La Mesa interchange
project. As such, you participate in development team meetings on a monthly basis and
provide input on project development procedures and standards that apply to capital
improvements on state highway facilities.
In light of your involvement in the past and present in the La Mesa project, which
involves specific parties (including PB), you are not permitted to do work for PB with
regard to the La Mesa interchange project. Moreover, because of your supervisory role at
Caltrans, the prohibition extends to any projects that are pending before your agency and
which was under your supervisory authoity atthe time you leave Calhans.2 (Reg.
18741.1, subd. (aX+).)
Questions 2 and 3
You ask about additional projects that may or may not be funded by with state
funds. You indicate these are existing contracts. To the extent these are contracts on
which you worked, you are subject to the permanent ban as discussed above. To the
extent these projects are not ones on which you worked or participated as a supervisor,
you are subject to the one-year ban as outlined above onpage 4.
For instance, under the one-year ban, you may not, as a paid anployee of PB,
appear before or communicate with the Caltrans, if the communication or appearance is
made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action of Caltrans,
or influencing any discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
2 "supervisory authoriQ/'is not a phrase defined under the Act. This phrase was adopted by the
Commission inthe Brov,tn Advice l*tter, No. A-91-033. There, an official was advised that he was
deemed to have personally and substantially participated in all proceedings of his former agency, if those
proceedings were in his chain of command during his tenure at the agency.lnln re Lucas (2000) 14 FPPC
Ops. 15, the Commission clarified that not all proceedings subordinate to an official within his or her chain
of command are considered 'hnder his or her supervisory authority." (Reg. 18741.1, subd. (a)(4).) The
Commission concluded that an official's general administrative oversight of a program to be carried out by
those subordinate to the official on an agency's organizational chart was insufficient to rise to the level of
"personal and substantial" involvement required by the Act. Because your position seems more analogous
to that of the official inthe Brown advice letter than that of the high-ranking official it In re Lucas, itis
likely that the projects under your supervisory chain are subject to the permanent ban. Without more
specifics, however, we cannot advise regarding those projects.
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property." ($ 87406, subd. (d)(1); reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(5).) An appearance or
communication "is for the purpose of influencing if it is made for the principal purpose of
supporting, promoting, influencing, modifuing, opposing, delaying, or advancing the
action or proceeding." (Reg. 18746.2, subd. (a).)'
Not all communications to a former state administrative agency employer are
prohibited by the one-year ban; rather, the ban extends only to those communications for
the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action, or influencing any
discretionary act "involving the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a
permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." ($ 87406,
subd. (d)(1); reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(5).) An appearance or communication before a
former state administrative agency employer, made as part of "services performed to
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, ffffit,
contract, or sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-year] prohibitions . . .
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of
any of these actions orproceedings." (Reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(5)(A); Quiring Advice
Letter, No. 4-03-272; Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.) If your communications with
personnel of Caltrans will be for the purpose of administering, implernenting, or fulfilling
the requirements of a pre-existing contract or project between PB and Caltrans, they are
not prohibited by the one-year ban so long as the services do not involve the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, agreernent or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property. This is a fact-specific query. To emphasize, these
activities would be prohibited under the permanent ban if, while in state service, you
participated in the proceedings as discussed above.
Question 4.
You ask whether the post employment restrictions apply to the pursuit of new
contracts if no state funds are involved in the project, and whether it applies if there is or
is not a Caltrans ernployee on the consultant selection panel.
With respect to the pursuit of new contracts (by which we assume to mean
contracts that were not pending before you at the time you depart Caltrans, and thus to
which the permanent ban does not apply), such pursuits are governed by the one-year ban
described above in the discussion surrounding question number 2. Tlrepertinent
elernents, each of which must be met for the prohibition to apply, are:
"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which
means he or she has either permanently left state service or is on a
leave ofabsence.
3 The Commission has advised that a former agency official may draft communications or
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency so long as the former employee is not identified
in connection with the client's efforts to influence adminishative action. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-
321.)
"(2) The appearance or cofllmunication is made within 12 months after
leaving state of{ice or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation, for
the appearance or cofilmunication. However, a payment made for
necessary travel, meals, and accommodations received directly in
connection with voluntary services is not prohibited or limited by this
section.
"(4) The appearance or corlmunication is made on behalf of any
person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. An
appearance or communication made by a public official solely to
represent his or her personal interests, as defined in2 cal. code Regs.
Section 18702.4, subdivision (bxl), is not prohibited or limited by this
section.
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for thepurpose of
influencing, as defined in2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any
legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act involving
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant or contract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the
requirements of an existing permit, license, 8c,:mt, contract, or sale
agreement may be excluded from the prohibitions of this regulation,
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings.
However, the prohibitions of Government code Sections 87401 and
87402 may apply.
"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or
ernployee of any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official
worked for or represented during the 12 months before leaving state
office or employment. An ernployee loaned to an agency is deemed to
have worked for or represented that agency.
"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel,
and other operations are subject to the direction and control of any
agency described in subdivision (b)(6)(A). However, whether an
agency is provided technical assistance or legal advice, or is subject to
oversight by another agency pursuant to state law, are not factors to be
considered in determining whether an agency is subject to the direction
and control of another.


Page No. l0
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and
control of the Governor, if the official was a designated employee of
the Govemor's office during the 12 months before leaving state office
or employment." (Reg. 187 46.1.)
These criteria are best understood when one has a particular project or set of
circumstances in mind. You should evaluate such projects in the future against these
criteria and seek further guidance should you have particular questions"
Question 5.
Your final question seeks clarification on the application of thepermanent ban.
Specifically, you inquire whether it applies to projects in which you are "directly
involved" or'have ever been involved in at my current position level." The complete
answer to your question can be found in the answer to question one. To summarize here,
the permanent ban applies to projects in which you participated personally and
substantially by making, participating in the making, or influencing of a governmental
decision. ($$ 87401 and 87402; Reg. 18741.1.) Moreover, this applies not only to
projects you are involved in at your current level but any such projects in the history of
your state service.

CONCLUSION: 1. In light of your involvement in the past and present in the La Mesa pdect,
which involves specific parties including Parsons Brinkerhoff firm (PB), you are not
permitted to do work for PB with regard to the La Mesa interchange project as discussed
below. Moreove6, because of your supervisory role at Caltrans, the prohibition extends to
anyprojects that are pending before your agency and which was under your supervisory
authority and in which you had personal and substantial involvement (as discussed
below) at the time you leave Calkans.
2. arrrd 3. Assuming the permanent ban does not apply, you may work for PB on
these projects. However, under the one-yearban, you may not, as apaid employee of
PB, appearbefore or communicate with the Caltrans, if the communication or appearance
is made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action of Caltrans,
or influencing any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale orpurchase of goods or
property.
4. The answer to this question depends on the application in any grven
circumstance of the factors enumerated in regulation 18746.1, discussed below. These
criteria are best understood when one has a particular project or set of circumstances in
mind. You should evaluate such projects in the future against these criteria and seek
further guidance should you have particular questions.
5. The permanent ban applies to projects in which you participated personally
and substantially by making, participating in the making, or influencing of a
governmental decision. Moreover, this applies not only to projects you are involved in at
your current level but any such projects in the history ofyour state service.

Thomas 4546 Sunnyside Drive Riverside, CA 92506 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-05-089 Dear Mr. Thomas: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. You would like clarification what restrictions would be placed on you if you accept private employment as the project manager for the La Mesa interchange project. 2. You ask whether there are any restrictions in acting as the project manager for the consulting firm particularly in dealing with Caltrans staff. 3. Are there any restrictions in acting as the project manager for the consulting firm particularly on a State highway project if there is no direct contact with Caltrans staff for one year? 4. Are there any restrictions in acting as the project manager when there are no State funds used on the project and no Caltrans employees on the panel, however, the project is on the State highway system? 5. Are you permanently restricted from working on any projects in which you are directly involved in or have ever been involved in at your current position level? ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-05-089 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. In light of your involvement in the past and present in the La Mesa project, which involves specific parties including Parsons Brinkerhoff firm (PB), you are not permitted to do work for PB with regard to the La Mesa interchange project as discussed below. Moreover, because of your supervisory role at Caltrans, the prohibition extends to any projects that are pending before your agency and which was under your supervisory authority and in which you had personal and substantial involvement (as discussed below) at the time you leave Caltrans. 2. and 3. Assuming the permanent ban does not apply, you may work for PB on these projects. However, under the one-year ban, you may not, as a paid employee of PB, appear before or communicate with the Caltrans, if the communication or appearance is made for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action of Caltrans, or influencing any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. 4. The answer to this question depends on the application in any given circumstance of the factors enumerated in regulation 18746.1, discussed below.
